title: Casirivimab -- Indications
content: This being said it is still reasonable to consider this drug combination in patients who test positive for SAR-COV-2 infection but are hospitalized due to some other indication. Patients or parents/caregivers have the option to accept or refuse this combination drug treatment. If treated with the drug cocktail, patients should continue to self-isolate and use infection control measures such as wearing masks, social distancing, disinfecting surfaces, and maintaining hand hygiene. Monoclonal antibodies provide rapid protection against infection, and protection can last for weeks to months. For patients receiving passive antibody therapy (eg, mAbs), the recommendation is to defer COVID-19 vaccination for at least 90 days to avoid potential interference with the immune response. [7] Please review the latest Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and FDA recommendations on these medications. With new virus variants emerging, the indications and conditions for use are likely to change.

title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
content: Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors Baricitinib is an oral selective inhibitor of Janus kinase (JAK) 1 and JAK 2 currently indicated for moderate to severely active rheumatoid arthritis patients. Baricitinib was considered a potential treatment for COVID-19 based on its inhibitory effect on SARS-CoV-2 endocytosis in vitro and on the intracellular signaling pathway of cytokines that cause the late-onset hyperinflammatory state that results in severe illness. [115] [116] This dual inhibitory effect makes it a promising therapeutic drug against all stages of COVID-19. A multicenter observational, retrospective study of 113 hospitalized patients with COVID-19 pneumonia who received baricitinib combined with lopinavir/ritonavir (baricitinib arm, n=113) or hydroxychloroquine and lopinavir/ritonavir (control arm, n=78) reported significant improvement in clinical symptoms and 2-week mortality rate in the baricitinib arm compared with the control arm. Results from the ACTT-2 trial, a double-blind, randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating baricitinib plus remdesivir in hospitalized adult patients with COVID-19, reported that the combination therapy of baricitinib plus remdesivir was superior to remdesivir therapy alone in not only reducing recovery time but also accelerating clinical improvement in hospitalized patients with COVID-19, particularly who were receiving high flow oxygen supplementation or non-invasive ventilation. [117] Baricitinib, in combination with remdesivir, has been approved for clinical use in hospitalized patients with COVID-19 under a EUA issued by the FDA. The efficacy of baricitinib alone or in combination with remdesivir has not been evaluated in the SARS-CoV-2 variants, and there is limited data on the use of baricitinib with dexamethasone. Ruxolitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and 2 indicated for myeloproliferative disorders, polycythemia vera, and steroid-resistant GVHD. Similar to baricitinib, it has been hypothesized to have an inhibitory effect on cytokines' intracellular signaling pathway, making it a potential treatment against COVID-19. Results from a small prospective multicenter randomized controlled phase 2 trial evaluating the efficacy and safety of ruxolitinib reported no statistical difference from the standard of care. However, most patients demonstrated significant chest CT improvement and faster recovery from lymphopenia. [118] A large randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter trial (NCT04362137) is ongoing to assess ruxolitinib's efficacy and safety in patients with severe COVID-19. Tofacitinib is another oral selective inhibitor of JAK 1 and JAK3 that is indicated for moderate to severe RA, psoriatic arthritis, and moderate to severe ulcerative colitis. Given its inhibitory effect on the inflammatory cascade, it was hypothesized that its use could ameliorate the viral inflammation-mediated lung injury in patients with severe COVID-19. Results from a small randomized controlled trial that evaluated the efficacy involving 289 patients who were randomized to receive tofacitinib or placebo showed that tofacitinib led to a lower risk of respiratory failure or death (PMID:34133856).

title: Baricitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
content: Breastfeeding considerations: During trials, baricitinib was detected in rat milk. Although it is unknown whether baricitinib is present in human milk, breastfeeding mothers are advised against its use. Preferably, an alternative drug should be considered, especially when nursing a newborn or preterm infant. The manufacturer recommends refraining from nursing during therapy and for 4 days after the last dose. [16] Pediatric patients: The FDA has issued the EUA for baricitinib to treat COVID-19 in hospitalized pediatric patients aged 2 to 17 who require supplemental oxygen, ECMO, or mechanical ventilation. [17] Older patients: No specific dose adjustment for baricitinib has been recommended for the older population.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Continuing Education Activity
content: This activity enhances clinician understanding of deucravacitinib by reviewing indications, mechanisms of action, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, drug-to-drug interactions, use in specific patient populations, dosage forms and strengths, administration methods, adverse event profiles, contraindications, and other critical factors such as patient counseling. Healthcare professionals must understand how to identify suitable candidates, establish realistic treatment goals, and select appropriate administration methods for deucravacitinib therapy. This activity also emphasizes detecting and managing adverse reactions promptly and promoting collaboration among the interprofessional healthcare team to improve patient outcomes.

title: Deucravacitinib -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
content: Breastfeeding considerations: Data on the secretion of deucravacitinib in human milk, its effects on lactogenesis, and its effects on breastfed infants are not available. During animal studies, deucravacitinib was found in the milk of rats. While prescribing deucravacitinib, the health benefits of breastfeeding infants and the clinical need for therapy must be weighed against the potential adverse effects on the breastfed child. [3] Pediatric patients: The safety and effectiveness of deucravacitinib have not been established in the pediatric patient population.

title: Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
content: Other pertinent studies include trials that supported the first-line treatment with imatinib and dosing including the CML Study IV, [13] the Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Optimization and Selectivity (TOPS) study, [14] and the International Randomized Study of Interferon and STI571 trial (IRIS). [22] Important trials concerning the efficacy and dosing of the second generation FDA-approved tyrosine kinase inhibitors include Bosutinib Efficacy and Safety in Newly Diagnosed CML trial (BELA), [15] Bosutinib Versus Imatinib in Adult Patients With Newly Diagnosed Chronic Phase Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia trial (BFORE), [23] Dasatinib Versus Imatinib Study in Treatment-Naive Chronic Myeloid Leukemia Patients trial (DASISION), [16] and the ENESTnd study. [17] Pertinent studies and ongoing trials assessing tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy discontinuation efficacy and candidate recommendations include the imatinib discontinuation study (STIM1), [24] Evaluating Nilotinib Efficacy and Safety in Clinical Trials: Treatment-Free Remission study (ENESTfreedom), [25] Imatinib Suspension and Validation study (ISAV), [26] and Discontinuation of Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor Therapy in Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia trial (EURO-SKI). [27]

title: Deucravacitinib -- Adverse Effects
content: Infection: Deucravacitinib may increase the risk of infections. In the placebo-controlled 16-week period, infections were reported in 29% of the patients treated with deucravacitinib (116 events per 100 person-years) compared with 22% treated with the placebo (83.7 events per 100 person-years). Most infections were not serious, were in the mild to moderate severity category, and did not cause discontinuation of deucravacitinib therapy. During this same period, serious infections occurred in 5 patients (2.0 per 100 patient-years) in the deucravacitinib group and 2 patients (1.6 per 100 patient-years) in the placebo group. During the 52-week treatment period, pneumonia and COVID-19 were the most common serious infections reported. [3]

title: Strategies for Individualizing Pulmonary Hypertension Treatment to Ensure Optimal Patient Outcomes (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Treatments
content: Selonsertib is a first-in-class small-molecule (apoptosis signal-regulating kinase 1 [ASK1]) inhibitor developed to treat diseases with a high burden of oxidative stress, including PAH. [99] The randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2 ARROW trial included 151 study participants. [100] All were aged 18 to 75 years and had a diagnosis of PAH. From December 2014 to November 2015, patients were stratified by PAH etiology and background therapy, and underwent random assignment to selonsertib 2 mg, selonsertib 6 mg, selonsertib 18 mg, or placebo administered once daily via tablet. The primary outcome was a change in PVR from baseline to 24 weeks, as measured by RHC. Mean change in PVR was 6 dynes/cm in the placebo group, 35 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 2-mg group ( P = .21), 28 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 6-mg group ( P = .27) and –21 dynes/cm in the selonsertib 18-mg group ( P = .60). [100] Additionally, the investigators reported no improvement in secondary outcomes such as exercise capacity, symptom burden, or worsening clinical events.

title: Adenoid Cystic Cancer -- Medical Oncology
content: Because ACCs do not have a fast turnover rate, systemic chemotherapy does not confer benefit generally, and therefore a standard chemotherapy regimen is not a part of treatment for ACCs. [1] However, the consensus remains to reserve chemotherapy for palliative purposes in patients with symptomatic metastatic disease, recurrent disease, or rapidly progressing tumors not amenable to surgery. Studies have also been done to look at possible molecular targets for immunotherapy agents, but results have been disappointing. Clinical trials looking at c-KIT (an onco-receptor overexpressed by ACCs) as a potential target for the c-KIT inhibitor, imatinib, showed disappointing results. [14] [15] Similarly, drugs targeting the EGFR and HER-2 receptor families did not show any clinical benefit. [16] [17] [18] However, a recent phase II trial looking at the tyrosine kinase inhibitor axitinib in patients with incurable recurrent/metastatic ACC showed that 66.7% of patients had tumor shrinkage and 9.1% of patients had a partial response suggesting that patients with 4q12 amplified ACCs may benefit from tyrosine kinase inhibitors. [19] NTRK inhibitors such as larotrectinib should be considered in NTRK mutations in the absence of other available options in recurrent or metastatic tumors, as approved by the FDA.

title: Nintedanib -- Indications
content: Nintedanib is a small molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) that binds to a family of growth factor receptors and prevents the proliferation of fibroblasts. This medication impedes the ongoing fibrotic process and delays the progression to long-lasting damage. Nintedanib was initially approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2014 as a treatment for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF). [1] Subsequently, nintedanib was noted to have antifibrotic effects in animal models of rheumatoid arthritis-associated interstitial lung disease (RA-ILD) and systemic sclerosis-associated interstitial lung disease (SSc-ILD). Interest in this antifibrotic property led to clinical trials demonstrating significant slowing of fibrosis in ILD related to systemic autoimmune rheumatic diseases (SARD), including systemic sclerosis. [2] Study results also indicated that nintedanib therapy slows the decline of forced vital capacity (FVC). [3] For patients with IPF, nintedanib significantly improves the absolute change in FVC from baseline compared to placebo. Nintedanib also demonstrated greater effectiveness for patients with a decline in FVC ≥10% compared to placebo. [4]

title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors, such as acalabrutinib, ibrutinib, and rilzabrutinib, are tyrosine kinase inhibitors that regulate macrophage signaling and activation currently FDA-approved for some hematologic malignancies. It is proposed that macrophage activation occurs during the hyperinflammatory immune response seen in severe COVID-19. Results from a small off-label study of 19 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 who received acalabrutinib highlighted the potential clinical benefit of BTK inhibition. [119]  Clinical trials are in progress to validate the efficacy in severe COVID-19 illness.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 75
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein‑kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, comprise dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي), whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). The “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” group, encompassing cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin, belongs to the same protein‑kinase‑inhibitor class and is employed to inhibit kinase signaling in cancer and to modulate immune responses. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically recognized for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Collectively, these agents illustrate how distinct kinase‑targeted drugs are organized within broader therapeutic families and linked to the management of various hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 48
content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subgroup of protein kinase inhibitors classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are BCR‑ABL inhibitors used for myeloid leukemia. Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, include ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, comprise canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, both belonging to the same antineoplastic and immunomodulating class. Cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin are members of the ATC subgroup “Other protein kinase inhibitors,” also classified as antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and gilteritinib and midostaurin have activity against acute myeloid leukemia. Collectively, these agents illustrate how precise molecular targeting of BCR‑ABL, BTK, EGFR and other kinases provides distinct therapeutic options across lymphoid, myeloid and chronic lymphocytic leukemias, underscoring the role of targeted kinase inhibition in modern oncology care.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
content: The community’s knowledge base defines leukemia (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a hematologic malignancy comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—share the mechanism of inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine‑kinase fusion protein and are assigned to specific subtypes: dasatinib, its anhydrous form, and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Additional anti‑leukemic agents include the corticosteroid derivative kortizon acetát, dexamethasone phosphate, the topoisomerase‑I inhibitor irinotecan hydrochloride, the antimetabolite methotrexate sodium, the alkylating agent pipobroman, and several prednisolone salts (prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, prednisolone tebutate). Conversely, the oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec is contraindicated in patients with any form of leukemia. These relationships support precise drug selection and comprehensive management of hematologic oncology patients.
